notes: Babylon. A symbol of the world and wickedness: "Go ye out from among the nations, even from Babylon, from the midst of wickedness, which is spiritual Babylon" (D&C 133:14).
burden massa' "something lifted up" against the wicked, with a call for their repentance

quotes.text: Burden is used several times in Isaiah's prophetic addresses to several of Israel's neighboring nations and to Jerusalem itself in the next dozen chapters. It is used to translate the Hebrew massa, meaning "something lifted up" against the wicked, with a call for their repentance. While addressed to nations known in history, the messages have symbolic meanings also for peoples having like faults in later times.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 513

notes: unto them --unto the Medes (Isa 13:17), the assailants of Babylon. It is remarkable that Isaiah does not foretell here the Jews' captivity in Babylon, but presupposes that event, and throws himself beyond, predicting another event still more future, the overthrow of the city of Israel's oppressors. It was now one hundred seventy-four years before the event.

It is remarkable that Isaiah does not foretell here the Jews' captivity in Babylon, but presupposes that event, and throws himself beyond, predicting another event still more future, the overthrow of the city of Israel's oppressors. It was now one hundred seventy-four years before the event.
Anciently, Israel's families and clans gathered with their own banner around the temple of Jehovah (Numbers 2:2; Mosiah 2:5-6). (Parry, Understanding Isaiah, 130).
high mountain (Heb. shaphah) rather, "a bare (literally, "bald," that is, without trees) mountain". Ensign Peak fits this description.

Jehovah's sanctified ones (Joshua 3:5) are those who are temple worthy, who actually attend the temple, and who are made holy by Christ's power. In ancient Israel the soldiers prepared for the holy war by participating in rituals connected with the temple (Deuteronomy 23:10-15) (Parry, Understanding Isaiah, 131).
sanctified ones [temple worthy - In ancient Israel the soldiers prepared for the holy war by participating in rituals connected with the temple (Deuteronomy 23:10-15)]

Those who join the Lord's army will be gathered from all the nations of the earth. "Yea, verily I say unto you again, the time has come when the voice of the Lord is unto you: Go ye out of Babylon; gather ye out from among the nations, from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other" (D&C 133:7; JS
far country --Media and Persia, stretching to the far north and east

quotes.text: Howl ye This recurring expression of Isaiah (16:7; 23:1, 6, 14; 65:14) implies that the wicked (those who belong to Babylon) are like dogs and wolves who howl under distress.

notes: flames [bright red shame and guilt that evil persons are experiencing or be inflamed because of weeping]
2 Ne. 23:8 omits the phrase they shall be in pain as a woman that travaileth
the arrogancy of the proud to cease, and will lay low the haughtiness of the terrible.
Judgment on Babylon: The Wicked Are Punished at the Second Coming (13:11-22)
Ophir "reducing to ashes" known for the highest quality of gold in the ancient Near East

A hunted deer is endangered when hunters are present, and sheep are endangered when shepherds are absent. The imagery implies that the wicked will be like a hunted deer; they will flee for their lives during this time (there will be wars - Matt. 24:6), and they will be like unprotected sheep, meaning those who have not accepted Jesus as their shepherd may not receive his protection.

quotes.text: Historically, the Medes, having formed an alliance with the Persians under Cyrus the Great's leadership, conquered the great Babylonian empire by damming the Euphrates River, marching through its riverbed, going under the city walls, and subsequently capturing Babylon. This event took place more than one hundred sixty years after Isaiah's prophecy. The phrase shall not regard silver and gold indicates that the Medes did not go into battle to obtain plunder (silver and gold); instead, their motivation was to kill, and gain power and control. Perhaps ancient Media points forward to all nations of the last days who have the same wicked designs.

notes: Sodom and Gomorrah [the greatest city in the world would not only be destroyed, but it would also never be rebuilt It is tantamount to saying Rome will lie in dust and never be inhabited or New York or some other equally as important and well situated city. It is one thing to predict the destruction of a city and another to foresee its complete disappearance and the location itself be desolate forever!]

beauty of . . . excellency --Hebrew, "the glory of the pride" of the Chaldees; it was their glory and boast. as . . . Gomorrah--as utterly (Jeremiah 49:18; 50:40; Amos 4:11). Taken by Cyrus, by clearing out the canal made for emptying the superfluous waters of the Euphrates, and directing the river into this new channel, so that he was able to enter the city by the old bed in the night.

neither . . . Arabian pitch tent --Not only shall it not be a permanent residence, but not even a temporary resting-place. The Arabs, through dread of evil spirits, and believing the ghost of Nimrod to haunt it, will not pass the night there (compare Isa 13:21). The region was once most fertile; but owing to the Euphrates being now no longer kept within its former channels, it has become a stagnant marsh, unfit for flocks.

wild beasts--Hebrew, tsiyim, animals dwelling in arid wastes. Wild cats, remarkable for their howl [BOCHART]. doleful creatures--"howling beasts," literally, "howlings" [MAURER]. owls--rather, "ostriches"; a timorous creature, delighting in solitary deserts and making a hideous noise [BOCHART]. satyrs--sylvan demi-gods--half man, half goat--believed by the Arabs to haunt these ruins; probably animals of the goat-ape species [VITRINGA]. Devil-worshippers, who dance amid the ruins on a certain night [J. WOLFF neither . . . shepherds--The region was once most fertile; but owing to the Euphrates being now no longer kept within its former channels, it has become a stagnant marsh, unfit for flocks; and on the wastes of its ruins (bricks and cement) no grass grows.
